coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have undergone extensive research and proved to have the potential to act both as drug delivery vehicles and imaging agents. Quinic acid is an antioxidant that has shown anticancer activity through apoptosis-mediated cytotoxicity in breast cancer cells. Besides, it has demonstrated a strong affinity for selectins, which are angiogenesis factors increased in breast cancer tissue. In this study, Nitrogen-doped CQDs were prepared via hydrothermal method. The resulting nanoparticles were conjugated with Quinic acid as targeting agent toward breast cancer cells. Characterization of the resulting nanoparticles included TEM, SEM, Zeta potential, FTIR, EDX, MAP, UV–Visual, and Fluorescent spectroscopy. Gemcitabine was loaded on the resulting nanoparticles through electrostatic interactions. Cell viability was evaluated via MCF7 cell line. In-vivo imaging and biodistribution studies were conducted in breast cancer cells growing in mice. Taken together, Quinic acid conjugated N-CQDs exhibited promising properties such as excellent luminescent properties and high tumor accumulation, suggesting that they could be excellent candidates as multifunctional theranostic agents.

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots- quinic acid for drug delivery of gemcitabine to breast cancer cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have undergone extensive research and proved to have the potential to act both as drug delivery vehicles and imaging agents. Quinic acid is an antioxidant that has shown anticancer activity through apoptosis-mediated cytotoxicity in breast cancer cells. Besides, it has demonstrated a strong affinity for selectins, which are angiogenesis factors increased in breast cancer tissue. In this study, Nitrogen-doped CQDs were prepared via hydrothermal method. The resulting nanoparticles were conjugated with Quinic acid as targeting agent toward breast cancer cells. Characterization of the resulting nanoparticles included TEM, SEM, Zeta potential, FTIR, EDX, MAP, UV–Visual, and Fluorescent spectroscopy. Gemcitabine was loaded on the resulting nanoparticles through electrostatic interactions. Cell viability was evaluated via MCF7 cell line. In-vivo imaging and biodistribution studies were conducted in breast cancer cells growing in mice. Taken together, Quinic acid conjugated N-CQDs exhibited promising properties such as excellent luminescent properties and high tumor accumulation, suggesting that they could be excellent candidates as multifunctional theranostic agents.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer has been reported to be the leading cause of cancer death among women in over 100 countries [1]. It is the second cause of cancer death after lung cancer concerning women worldwide [2]. Despite significant progress in surgery, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy, breast cancer still accounts for 15% of cancer deaths [3]. It is estimated that 15.3% of all new cancer diagnoses in 2020 will be associated with breast cancer in the United States [4]. Chemotherapy is one of the most effective treatments for breast cancer. However, the use of chemotherapeutic drugs is often hampered in clinical settings due to their grievous side effects [5]. As chemotherapeutic drugs diffuse into almost all of the organs, killing both tumor cells and normal, healthy cells [6]. The use of nanomaterials as effective drug delivery systems for cancer treatment has recently gained specific interest, and continuous research is focusing on this field to reduce the adverse effects caused by conventional methods [7]. In recent years, anticancer agents have been employed passively with nanostructure systems for the targeted treatment and diagnosis of breast cancers [8]. Implementing novel drug delivery systems have helped us protect drugs against metabolic inactivation, improve their plasma half-life, lower their side effects, and enhance both their therapeutic index and anticancer efficacy [9].

Gemcitabine (2′, 2′-difluorodeoxycytidine) is an antimetabolite drug that has been applied in the treatment of different cancers such as colon, pancreatic, lung, ovarian, and bladder neoplasms [10–14]. This drug has demonstrated satisfactory therapeutic outcomes in breast cancer both as a single agent therapy [15,16] and combination therapy [16–18]. As a pyrimidine analog, Gemcitabine incorporates into DNA structure, blocks DNA polymerase activity, disrupts DNA synthesis, and inhibits cell proliferation [19,20].

Gemcitabine (Gem) has a short plasma half-life (less than 1 h), and it quickly decomposed after administration. The short plasma half-life of Gem limits its efficacy drastically [21]. Therefore, high doses are required to achieve the desired therapeutic outcome, which limits its medical applicability [21,22]. To improve Gem's bioavailability and therapeutic efficacy, nanocarrier-based formulations have gained wide recognition as they facilitate controlled release and targeted delivery of drugs [23,24].

Various nanoparticles with multifunctional capabilities have been designed to integrate various functional materials into one multi-component nanoparticle. These nanoparticles have been applied as diagnostic and therapeutic agents for cancer targeting and imaging [25].

It has been predicted that at least one-third of the population of economically developed countries will be diagnosed with cancer at some point in their lives [26]. Accordingly, early cancer detection and diagnosis have become major priorities of the scientific community [27]. Multifunctional nano-theranostic materials that associate both therapeutic and diagnostic functions into one compartment are now gaining prominence in drug delivery design [28,29]. The desired novel drug carriers should have the ability to detect cancerous cells, picture their location, and destroy malignant cells without any harm to healthy cells [30].

Quantum dots (QDs) are light-emitting nanostructures that can act both as imaging agents and carriers for drugs, thereby functioning as a theranostic agent [31]. QDs are semiconductor nanocrystals [32], which emit fluorescence when excited with a light source. These nanoparticles are often composed of groups II-IV or III–V elements of periodic table [33]. The toxic heavy metal present in QDs restricts their use in living tissues even at low concentrations [34]. On the contrary, extensive research on Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) has imposed negligible toxicity. Since carbon is the primary element of all living organisms, full carbon nanomaterials have lower toxicity profiles in comparison with other nanomaterials [35]. Carbon nanostructures have found a very important niche in medical science. Li et al. synthesized a novel drug delivery system using carboxylated graphene quantum dots (cGQDs). In this research, Folic acid-poly(ethylene glycol)-cGQDs was successfully synthesized with desirable entrapment efficiency and drug loading capacity. Cell image analysis indicated that the nanosystem entered into human cervical cancer cells mainly via the macropinocytosis-dependent pathway. In vivo experiments manifested high tumor accumulation and antitumor ability [36]. In another study, Huang et al. synthesized CQDs derived from byproducts in bio-refinery process. The resulting nanoparticles had a size distribution from 2.0 to 6.0 nm with blue-green fluorescence. In addition, in vivo cell imaging and bioimaging experiments showed that the CQDs had low cytotoxicity, antioxidant activity, and an overall excellent bioimaging performance in-vivo [37]. CQDs are promising nanomaterials for biomedical applications in the field of bioimaging, biosensing, and biotherapy [38,39]. In another research conducted by Zavareh et al. synthesized 5-fluorouracil-chitosan-carbon quantum dot-aptamer (5-FU–CS–CQD-Apt) for the targeted delivery of 5-FU in breast cancer. The resulting system could kill MCF-7 cancer cells efficiently and could be used as a potential nanocarrier in breast cancer treatment [40]. As a result, the focus of studies has recently shifted from QD to CQDs. CQDs ubiquitous application results from their biocompatibility and safety profile, high quantum yield, aqueous solubility, facile synthesis, and excellent physicochemical stability and easy functionalization [34,41–43]. Studies indicate that surface passivation can prompt an increase in fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CQDs [44,45]. Utilizing this approach, CQDs have been doped with various heteroatoms, such as nitrogen [46]. Nitrogen doping with primary amines such as ethylenediamine has shown better results than secondary or tertiary amines [47]. Yang et al. reported highly fluorescent CQDs with QY as high as 80% using citric acid as starting material and ethylenediamine as the surface passivating agent [48].

Opening the door for numerous additional applications, CQDs are now being studied for optical imaging and drug delivery applications. Furthermore, if we can deliver our anti-cancer agents exclusively to tumor cells via targeting, the associated adverse effects can be minimized significantly [49]. E− and P-selectins are endothelial adhesion molecules. These molecules are overexpressed as a result of inflammation in cancerous tissues [50]. P- Selectins capture tumor cells at the endothelium and mediate tumor angiogenesis and metastasis [51] as well as cancer proliferation [52]. Studies show that both E-selectins [53–55] and P-selectins [56,57] are increased in breast cancer tissues. Nanoparticles with an affinity for selectins may bind to vasculature surrounding tumors (“peritumoral endothelium”) and extravasate into tumors beyond the level possible with the EPR effect. Sialyl Lewis-x (sLex) is a carbohydrate structure that binds to selectins and can be used as a ligand for drug carriers. We used quinic acid as a synthetic mimic of sLex in decorating our nanopartivles [58].

In this paper, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were established via a facile hydrothermal method. To design a selectin–targeted drug delivery system, Quinic acid molecules were attached on the surface of CQDs to facilitate the receptor-mediated uptake of the carrier by cancer cells. The morphology, structure, and properties of the N-CQDs-Quinic acid were investigated. Gem was loaded in the carriers by means of electrostatic interaction. Further, the blood circulation and tissue biodistribution profiles of the resulting N-CQDs-Quinic acid-Gem were determined following intravenous (i.v.) administration via the tail vein of mice.

Citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (≥99%) (EDA), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), Quinic acid, Gemcitabine, and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany).
All of the compounds were of analytical grade, and the solutions were achieved with ultrapure water.

A total 6 male Balb/c mice (20 ± 0.3 g), at 4–8 weeks of age were obtained from Tehran University of Medical Science. 4T1 tumor-bearing model was generated by subcutaneous injection of 2 × 106 4T1 cells in 0.2 mL of PBS into the flank of mice. 14–21 days after implantation, the tumor-bearing mice were subject to in vivo imaging studies. All animal experiments complied with the ARRIVE guidelines and were carried out in accordance with the U.K. Animals (Scientific Procedures) Act, 1986 and associated guidelines, EU Directive 2010/63/EU for animal experiments, and the National Institutes of Health guide for the care and use of laboratory animals (NIH Publications No. 8023, revised 1978). The experimental protocol was approved by the institutional ethical committee [Reg. No. IR.TUMS.TIPS.REC.1398.039] at Tehran University of Medical Science, Tehran, Iran.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CQDs and N-CQDs- quinic acid
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 displays a schematic illustration of the preparation process of N-CQDs using a microreactor. Briefly, 0.1 ml EDA and 1.8 g CA were slowly added into 20 ml ultrapure water and stirred for 30 min to ensure uniform dispersion. Then, the mixture was transferred into a 20 ml Teflon liner and heated in an autoclave for 6 h at 180 °C by a hydrothermal method. After the autoclave, it was cooled to room temperature; the reaction products were further dialyzed (1000 Da) to remove the residual reagents and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to separate them from agglomerated larger particles and excess impurities. To produce the covalently bonded N-CQDs- Quinic acid conjugates, the carboxyl groups on the surface of the N-CQDs were reacted with Quinic acid in the presence of EDC and NHS [59]. Generally, EDC and NHS were added to activate the carboxylic groups present on N-CQDs, so that the carboxyl groups could react with amine groups of Quinic acid to form amide bonds. First, 1 ml of 1 mM solution of EDC/NHS (EDC/NHS = 1:10) was added to 10 ml of N-CQDs solution and stirred for 2 h. Then, 1 ml of 1 mM solution of Quinic acid in water was added and stirred overnight. The as-obtained nanoparticles were finally purified via silica gel column chromatography and dialysis. The final yellow supernatant was collected, freeze-dried, and stored for further use.

The optical absorption spectra of samples were analyzed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Carry 100 Bio, Varian, Australia) over the wavelength range from 200 nm to 700 nm (5 nm interval). Fluorescence measurements were performed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Carry eclipse, Varian, Australia). Zeta potential measurements were done by a Malvern Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK). The size and morphology of the samples were evaluated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using CEM 902A (Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany). SEM (FE-SEM, Tescan/Mira, Brno, Czech Republic) was also employed to further characterize the shape and surface morphology of nanoparticles. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the N-CQDs and N-CQDs-quinic acid were recorded by the FT-IR spectrometer (PerkinElmer Spectrum BX-II spectrometer, Nizhni Novgorod, Russia) in KBr pellets over the wavenumber ranging from 400 to 4000 cm−1. Furthermore, the elemental composition of the carbon dots was determined by an electron dispersive X-ray study (EDX, JSM-6390LV) to confirm the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) in the resulting nanoparticles.

The effect of Gem addition on the fluorescence intensity of the N- CQDs-Quinic acid was evaluated. The fluorescence spectra were recorded from 400 nm to 640 nm. 5 ml diluted water solution of N- CQDs-Quinic acid was obtained (20 μg/ml), and After 5 min, the fluorescence intensity of the solution was measured by spectrofluorometer at excitation/emission wavelengths of 360/490 nm. The fluorescence of N- CQDs-Quinic acid was measured after the addition of 1 ml of Gem in various concentrations of 0–100 μM. The fluorescence measurements were performed after 5 min of Gem addition.

For encapsulation studies, 5 ml of N-CQD-Quinic acid solution (20 μg/ml) was mixed with 100 μL of different concentrations of Gem in water and rotated at 90 rpm for 24 h using a magnetic stirrer, inside light protected tubes at room temperature. After the incubation period, the nanoparticle suspension was centrifuged at 30,000 rpm for 20 min using Beckman 70Ti Ultracentrifuge to remove unencapsulated Gem. The encapsulation efficiency of N-CQD-Quinic acid- Gem was determined indirectly using the supernatant obtained during preparation, as mentioned above. One millimeter of supernatant collected by centrifugation of the NPs was used to estimate the amount of encapsulated drug by UV spectrophotometer. Entrapment efficiency (EE) was calculated by measuring the initial concentration and nonencapsulated Gem, according to the following formula:
100 %EE= ((Initial concentration-non-entrapped drug)/ Initial concentration) ×

The in vitro release of Gem from the N-CQD-Quinic acid-Gem dispersion was performed by the dialysis technique. Briefly, about 5 mL of N-CQD-Quinic acid- Gem (20 μg/ml) were transferred in a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 500 Da) and suspended in 35 ml of PBS solution (pH 7.4, simulating extracellular environment and pH 5 representing cancer environment) with continuous stirring in a magnetic stirrer (100 rpm) at 37 °C. About 1 ml of the sample was withdrawn from the mixture at specific time points, and 1 ml of fresh medium was replenished each time to maintain a sink condition [60]. The collected samples were centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 10 min and assayed spectrophotometrically at 268 nm to quantify the drug release [61].

MCF-7 breast cancer cell line was used for cell viability assay. The cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a concentration of 4000–5000 cells per well in a culture medium consisting of Dulbecco's modified eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 5 ml fetal bovine serum (FBS) 10%, 1% mixture of penicillin-streptomycin and 5 ml of glutamine and maintained at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere. After 24 h, when a partial monolayer was formed, increasing concentration of N- CQDs, N- CQDs- Quinic acid and N- CQDs- Quinic acid-Gem and Gem alone were added to each well and further incubated for 48 h. Untreated cells were considered as negative control and assigned as 100% viability. The cells were treated with 20 μl of MTT solution (0.5 mg/ml) and the plates were incubated for another 4 h, allowing the viable cells to transform the yellow MTT into dark-blue formazan crystals. The medium was removed and the intracellular formazan was solubilized in DMSO. The absorbance of formazan was evaluated by the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) microplate Reader at 570 nm. The intensity of the purple color indicates the conversion of MTT by the redox activity of living cells. The relative cell viability (CV) was determined by the following equation:
CV (%) = [A] test well/ [A] control well × 100%
Where [A]test well is the absorbance of the test sample, and [A]control well is the absorbance of control samples. The IC50 was determined for Gem and N-CQDs-Quinic acid- Gem, which was defined as the concentration preventing proliferation to a level equal to 50% of that of controls and samples. The statistical significance was determined by comparison of mean IC50 values for Gem and N-CQDs-Quinic acid- Gem using Student's t-test and GraphpadPrism7.

Male BALB/c nude mice were obtained from the laboratory animal center. A combination of ketamine and xylazine was used to anesthetize the mice. N-CQD Quinic acid-Gem was injected into mice intravenously via tail vein. Images were obtained in 15, 30, 60, and 120 min post-injection.

For histopathological analysis, the animals were sacrificed 24 h post-injection and the harvested tissues were fixed in 10% neutral-buffered formalin. After 48 h, the mouse organs were embedded in paraffin, sectioned, and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). The stained tissue sections from control and treatment groups were evaluated histologically by light microscopy (Olympus BX51; Olympus, Tokyo, Japan).

Statistical data analysis was carried out with GraphPad Prism 7 software. For quantitative data analysis, One Way ANOVA was performed, followed by Kruskal- Wallis test. The IC50 values were generated from the MTT results using GraphPad.

Citric acid, as a common carbon source, was used to synthesize N-CQD-Quinic acid in this study. The N-CQD-Quinic acid dispersion was yellow and transparent under daylight and showed strong bright blue luminescence under UV light (Fig. 2 c).

The spectral properties of N-CQD-Quinic acid were investigated by UV–vis absorption and PL spectra in water solution. As shown in Fig. 2b, UV–vis absorption spectra exhibit obvious absorption bands at 265 nm, which are mainly ascribed to the π- π transition of CC double bond, and the absorption peaks located around 325 nm are due to the transition of n- π bonds of CO [62,63].
The PL emission spectrum has a strong peak at 490 nm, and the PL excitation has a band that peaked at 360 nm when 490 nm PL was detected.

The quantum yield of the N-CQD-Quinic acid samples were determined following slope method [38], where quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 was chosen as the reference (literature quantum yield = 0.54 at 360 nm). The quantum yield of the samples were as high as 51.64%.

The stability and surface charge of the particles were evaluated by Zeta potential. According to the literature, non-passivated CQDs display a negative zeta potential because of the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on their surface [64]. However, The N-CQDs prepared in this work show less negative zeta potential due to the conversion of carboxylic groups into amines. The obtained results show that the Zeta potential is −1.73 ± 0.5 mV for N-CQDs and −0.1 ± 0.1 mV for N-CQD-Quinic acid. There was a slight difference between the Zeta potential of N-CQDs and N-CQD-Quinic acid. The zeta potential plays a crucial role in the stability of nanoparticles [65]. In this paper, the stability of the nanoparticles was investigated with the help of zeta potential values measured three and six months after synthesis. The lyophilized N-CQD-Quinic acid was dispersed in distilled water and sonicated for 5 min and then the measurements were taken in triplicate at 25 °C. the resulting values remained without significant alteration, as it can be seen in Table 1 .

In terms of dispersion stability, 1 ml of N-CQD-Quinic acid solution (20 μg/ml) was added to 5 ml of deionized water and mice plasma and stored for up to 7 days. The PL intensity of the solutions at 0, 1 and 7 days after storage at 4 °C was evaluated and the I/I0 ratio was calculated and is presented in Fig. 3 d. The I/I0 ratio for the solutions remained over 90% during the storage, indicating that the dispersion N-CQD-Quinic acid was stable under standard storage conditions.

In order to obtain information about the nanoparticles’ morphology (shape and dimension), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used at first. According to the SEM image of N-CQD-Quinic acid, samples were found to be with spherical shape, with an average size of 7.55 ± 1.04 nm, which was additionally confirmed with TEM image. The TEM image in Fig. 4 c reveals that the resulting N-CQD-Quinic acid is uniform and well-dispersed with a narrow size distribution. These particles displayed a spherical morphology with an average diameter of (6.948 ± 0.699) nm.

Fig. 5 presents the high angle annular dark field (HAADF) scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and the elemental mapping of nanoparticles, which revealed the presence of C, N, and O in N-CQDs (a) and N-CQDs- Quinic acid nanoparticles (b).

As shown in the EDX quantitative spectrum, the sample contains a strong atomic percentage of C, O, and N. The atomic percentage of C, N, and O in N-CQDs were found 43.93, 34.17 and 21.90% and the atomic percentage of C, N, and O in N-CQD-Quinic acid were 51.83, 20.75 and 27.68%, respectively. The EDX results indicate that the surface of the as-synthesized N-CQDs and N-CQD-Quinic acid is functionalized by multiple carbon, oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups.

The structural information of N-CQDs and N-CQDs-Quinic acid were characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 6
. The stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H are located at 3400–3500 cm−1 [66]. These functional groups improve the hydrophilicity and stability of the N-CQDs in aqueous system [63]. The stretching vibrations of N–H and C–N = at 1440 cm−1 confirm the successful surface passivation and doping of nitrogen. The narrow peak located at ~2800 cm−1 suggests the stretching vibrations of C–H [67]. In the N-CQD sample, the peak at 1730 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO [68]. Whereas, in N-CQD-Quinic acid, a sharp peak can be seen at 1600 cm−1, which can be attributed to amide groups formed after the conjugation of Quinic acid with N-CQDs [69], so it can be concluded that proper conjugation has taken place. The FTIR and EDX results are coincident, both confirming that nitrogen was incorporated into the structure of CQDs and the resulting N-CQDs are nitrogen-doped and carbon-rich nanodots (see Fig. 7).

To study the effect of Gem addition on the fluorescence intensity of the N- CQDs-Quinic acid, different concentrations of Gem were added, and the intensity of emission at about 490 nm decreased (Fig. 6). The quenching effects of N- CQDs-Quinic acid by addition of Gem could be described by the well-known Stern–Volmer equation [70]:
I0/I = 1 + Ksv [Gem]
Where I0 is the fluorescence intensity before the addition of Gem, I is the fluorescence intensity after the addition of Gem and KSV is the quenching constant.

The relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity and concentration of GEM was found to be linear with a correlation coefficient of 0.9709. This suggests that the synthesized N-CQDs- Quinic acid were highly sensitive towards the addition of Gem.

For encapsulation studies, the amount of Gem encapsulated in N-CQDs-Quinic acid was determined as the difference between the amount of drug added during the N-CQDs-Quinic acid- Gem preparation and the amount of drug present in the supernatant. The encapsulation efficiency was expressed as the percentage of the total amount of Gem that was encapsulated. Different amounts of Gem was added for encapsulation studies. The encapsulation efficacy reached 21% when 500 μg/ml of Gem was added, which was used for further analyses (Table 2 ). This amount is less than the amount of drug needed to quench the fluorescent effect of N-CQD-Quinic acid significantly.

The drug content was estimated by UV spectrophotometer at 268 nm in PBS at different pH values (pH 7.4 representing the physiological environment, and pH 5 representing the cancer environment). The cumulative release of Gem can be seen in Fig. 8 . The drug release followed a first-order kinetic. The initial release of Gem can be resultant from the dissolution of drug crystals on the surface of nanoparticles. Electrostatic interactions between protonated amino residues on Gem and anionic group are responsible for the surface-bound interactions involved in the burst release. Studies have demonstrated that the plasma half-life of Gem after intravenous administration is about 30 min [71]. In this research, the best-fit nonlinear regression was obtained by GraphpadPrism, and the release half-life was calculated by 0.693/k. (t1/2 release = 2.726 h for pH: 7.4 and t1/2 release = 3.884 h for pH: 5) and the plasma half-life of Gem was improved after using N-CQD- Quinic acid.

The cumulative release reached the plateau of the curve at 53.29% within 51.1 h, and the drug release profile reached 54.01% after 72 h in the physiological environment. Within acidic pH, the cumulative release reached the plateau of the curve at approximately 79.52% within 38.9 h and the drug release profile reached 81.01% after 72 h. The mechanism of drug release at acidic pH was hypothesized reasonable in this system, which was attributed to the protonation of Gem and its enhanced hydrophilicity at acidic conditions [72]. The release is higher in pH 5, which shows the specificity of N-CQD-Quinic acid-Gem for cancer cells and less harm to the normal cells. The drug release profile in pH = 7.5 and pH = 5 are significantly different. Therefore it can be concluded that pH dependent delivery of Gem could be developed through the use of this nanosystem.

To exploit the resulting nanoparticles in biological applications, their effect on cell viability was evaluated. The MTT (3 -(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay is a simple colorimetric assay to measure the mitochondrial function to evaluate cell viability after treatment with the developed formulations. The cytotoxicity study was performed using MCF-7 (human breast adenocarcinoma cancer cell lines).

The comparative cytotoxicity was determined by presuming untreated control owing to 100% viability. MTT assays of cell viability studies suggested that the N-CQDs and N-CQDs-Quinic acid exhibited low cytotoxicity and posed insignificant toxic effects. These results suggested that these nanoparticles can be used in high concentrations for imaging or other biomedical applications. The viability of the cells varies between 80 and 100% after 48 h of incubation, with N-CQDs in the concentration range of 5–50 μg/ml (Fig. 9 a and b), suggesting that the dots had no significant cytotoxic effect on cells. For Gem and N-CQD-Quinic acid- Gem group, the cell viability decreased with the increase of the concentrations. The anticancer activity N-CQDs-Quinic acid-Gem as a whole was as high as free Gem. Therefore, the cytotoxicity of Gem could be significantly improved over the free drug by using the nano-formulation. The cytotoxicity of targeted formulations may have been caused by receptor-mediated endocytosis of the nanoparticles [73]. The N-CQDs-Quinic acid complexes could help Gem accumulate in the cells and thus significantly improve the cytotoxicity and the therapeutic effects (Fig. 9c and d). As shown in Fig. 10 , complete dose-response curves were generated and IC50 values were calculated. The IC50 values for Gem and N-CQDs-Quinic acid- Gem were 4.102 μg/ml and 2.908 μg/ml, respectively. The ability of drugs to induce apoptosis could be evaluated by IC50. That is, the lower IC50 values, the stronger ability to induce cell apoptosis [74]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the N-CQDs-Quinic acid- Gem causes relatively more toxic effects as compared with free Gem. As nanosized systems, penetrate in the tissue system, assist drug uptake by cells, permit an efficient drug delivery, and ensure action at the targeted location [75].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging (biodistribution of N- CQDs- quinic acid-gem in BALB/c mice)
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs have demonstrated outstanding potential as bioimaging agents because of their biocompatibility and low biotoxicity [76]. In this study, an aqueous solution of N-CQD-Quinic acid-Gem (20 μg/mL) (at pH 7.2) was used for in vivo fluorescence imaging. 300 μL of this solution was given by tail vein injection to live BALB/c mice for whole-body circulation. The mice were imaged with excitation at 360 nm using an in vivo fluorescence imaging system. Initially, the mice were imaged for 2 h. Animals were sacrificed 2 h post-injection. Optical images of the distribution of the N-CQD-Quinic acid-Gem in the tumor-bearing mouse over increasing times are shown in Fig. 11 . After 15 min, an optical signal could be detected in different parts of the body, mainly in the intestinal regions and cleared from the body through the urinary tract as the kidneys are the main organs for foreign particle clearance from the blood. As time progressed, the N-CQD-Quinic acid-Gem circulated in the mouse's body and gradually congregated at the location of the tumor. This observation indicates that the N-CQD-Quinic acid-Gem cycle in a mouse's body and also aggregate where it is intended to, almost exclusively at the location of the tumor.

The bio-distribution of N-CQD-Quinic acid-Gem was further evaluated using ex vivo fluorescence imaging, which was performed on the excised major organs. The dissected gastrointestinal tract and genitourinary tract exhibited meaningful fluorescence from the carbon dots. Fig. 12 shows that the dissected kidney, prostate, testis, bladder, and tumor from the euthanized mouse, which all showed strong fluorescence intensity. The accumulation of nanoparticles in the kidneys suggests the excretion of N-CQD-Quinic acid-Gem from the urinary tract and the result of clearance is confirmed by the presence of N-CQD-Quinic acid-Gem nanoparticles in the bladder [77]. No fluorescence signals were spotted in the organs of the heart, lungs, and spleen. Ex vivo evaluation of excised organs showed that the compound was mainly taken up by the tumor, which correlated well with our in vivo imaging results. Overall, most of the N-CQD-Quinic acid-Gem were accumulated in the tumor, indicating that N-CQD-Quinic acid-Gem has great potential to be used as a viable bioimaging agent. The physicochemical parameters of nanomaterials, such as size, shape, charge and functional groups, substantially influence their biodistribution and clearance in vivo [78]. The large nanoparticles will inevitably be caught by the liver, either by hepatocytes (for further biliary excretion in faeces) or Kupffer cells (resident phagocytes of the liver) [79,80]. However, nanoparticles capable of being synthesized <8 nm in diameter, such as quantum dots, can undergo renal clearance if other parameters, like surface charge, is optimized for this excretion pathway [81]. Negatively charged plasma proteins are adsorbed onto the positively charged nanoparticles and facilitate their capture by liver and spleen. So, a positive charge often results in the uptake of the nanoparticles by macrophage-rich organs (liver and spleen). Such an uptake leads to a faster elimination for positively charged particles than for negative or neutral particles [79].

Studies on the actual size cutoff of renal clearance vary in their results [82]. Some studies have reported the biodistribution of CQDs in mice after intravenous (iv.) administration, although further studies are required in this context and the available data are inconclusive. In vivo fluorescence imaging provides only a qualitative, semiquantitative measurement of quantum dot behavior in vivo, further research needs to be conducted to label the CQDs surface covalently with a gamma ray–emitting isotope and study the CQDs distribution precisely [80].

To explore the effect of N-CQD-Quinic acid on the organs of mice, a histological assay was conducted covering major organs of liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and tumor. A remarkable degree of safety was confirmed through histopathological evaluation. As shown in Fig. 13 , in all the studied tissues, including liver, spleen, lung, and kidney, there were no apparent abnormalities or pathological changes observed upon treatment. The morphological architecture of the liver, spleen, lung, and kidney showed almost identical morphological similarity in the treated and non-treated group. The N-CQD-Quinic acid nanoparticles caused no pathological changes in the form of diminished and distorted glomeruli, dilated tubules, edema exudate, and necrosis in the kidney. No pathological changes in the spleen were observed in the form of distorted lymphoid architecture, minimized lymphoid follicles, and diffused white pulp. No form of steatosis and the abundance of micro and macro vesicles were observed in the liver. In the lungs, there were no changes in the alveolar wall thickness and no observation of patchy interstitial inflammation with edema and neutrophil infiltration into the alveolar spaces.

Carbon dots were successfully prepared with citric acid and ethylenediamine by means of a hydrothermal method. The as-obtained N-CQD-Quinic acid exhibited a strong emission at 490 nm with an optimum excitation at 360 nm. The N-CQD-Quinic acid-Gem drug delivery system showed a relatively high loading capacity and a good drug release profile. The N-CQDs possessed good biocompatibility. The in vitro results demonstrated that the N-CQD-Quinic acid-Gem complex had a higher cellular cytotoxicity and more effective inhibition capacity on cancer cells than free Gem. N-CQD-Quinic acid-Gem showed great potential as fluorescent nanomaterials for in vivo non-invasive imaging. Taken together, it could be concluded that the N-CQD- Gem nanodrugs may hold great potential for cancer imaging and chemotherapeutic applications.

Shabnam Samimi: Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Mehdi Shafiee Ardestani: Conceptualization, Resources, Methodology, Supervision. Farid Abedin Dorkoosh: Supervision.
